data.title: Digital Realty forecasts $8.15-$8.20 2026 core FFO per share excluding promote, backed by $4.25B-$4.75B capex plan
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Digital Realty Trust (DLR) Q2 2026

Management view

  • "Digital Realty's momentum accelerated in the second quarter with record core FFO per share, supporting another increase to our full year guidance." (President, CEO & Director Andrew Power)

  • Management highlighted record leasing signals across product pillars, including "$108 million of bookings in our 0-1 megawatt plus interconnection business" and said the quarter delivered "a record 25-plus percent" renewal spread. (Senior VP of Public & Private Investor Relations Jordan Sadler)

  • CEO Andrew Power said hyperscale demand remained broad-based and noted post-quarter activity: "Subsequent to quarter end, we signed 2 additional hyperscale leases in the U.S., representing another $410 million of annualized GAAP base rent at 100% share or $205 million at Digital Realty share." (President, CEO & Director Power)

  • Management detailed strategic actions tied to its three pillars, including an expanded development runway and private capital scaling. CEO Andrew Power said the company "announced the expansion into the Kansas City Metro, securing 600 megawatts of utility power that begins to ramp in early 2028 with a long-term runway of up to 2 gigawatts of utility power" and added the planned acquisition of Columbia Capital "will meaningfully scale our private capital platform, adding more than $9 billion of fund commitments." (President, CEO & Director Power)

  • "Digital Realty delivered double-digit growth across virtually every major operating and financial metrics during the second quarter" and ended the quarter with leverage "at 4.7x." (Chief Financial Officer Matt Mercier)

Outlook

  • "We are raising our 2026 core FFO per share guidance, excluding net promote income by $0.15 at the low end and $0.10 at the high end to a new range of $8.15 to $8.20 per share." (Chief Financial Officer Mercier)

  • Management raised several operating assumptions, including: "cash renewal spreads of 9% to 11%" and "Same capital cash NOI growth of 4.25% to 5.25% on a constant currency basis" plus higher investment: "CapEx, net of partner contributions is expected to increase by $750 million from last quarter to $4.25 billion to $4.75 billion." (Chief Financial Officer Mercier)

  • CFO Matt Mercier added capital recycling expectations were lifted: "we have added another $500 million to our dispositions and JV capital guidance." (Chief Financial Officer Mercier)

Financial results

  • The company reported core FFO of $2.65 per share, including net promote income, and said, "Excluding net promote income, core FFO was a record $2.13 per share, up 14% year-over-year." (Chief Financial Officer Mercier)

  • Management reported a larger contracted revenue runway, stating, "Our total backlog reached a new record of $1.9 billion" and that in 2H 2026, "$635 million of annualized rent is scheduled to commence." (Chief Financial Officer Mercier)

  • CFO Matt Mercier described notable one-time and episodic items impacting quarterly earnings, including: "we received $113 million of proceeds" tied to an insurance recovery, and "Digital Realty realized roughly $200 million of promote income associated with the Blackstone transaction." (Chief Financial Officer Mercier)

  • On organic growth, the company reported: "Same capital cash NOI growth strengthened further in the second quarter, increasing 8.9% year-over-year" and added, "On a constant currency basis, same capital cash NOI increased 7.2%." (Chief Financial Officer Mercier)

Q&A

  • Eric Luebchow, Wells Fargo: asked how management underpins "multiple years of 10% growth" amid higher CapEx and deal accretion; Chief Financial Officer Mercier said the growth algorithm combines "renewal execution," hyperscale leasing and backlog, "another record in our 0-1 and interconnection demand," and private capital that provides "fee income."

  • Nicholas Del Deo, MoffettNathanson: asked how the asset mix may shift across colo/connectivity, on-balance sheet hyperscale, and off-balance sheet vehicles; President, CEO & Director Power said the company is growing colo/connectivity largely "on balance sheet," cited "142 new logos this quarter," and described hyperscale momentum with "$1.4 billion of signings" year-to-date including early July.

  • Michael Rollins, Citi: asked about development/power timing and a framework for fee income scaling; President, CEO & Director Power cited "north of $20 billion of projects under development" and "63-and-change percent pre-leased," while Chief Financial Officer Mercier said normalized fee income was "around $45 million" in Q2 and tied scaling to "$10 billion, $12 billion of capital" deployable in private capital structures.

  • Madison Rezaei, Bernstein: asked if AI broadening is changing hyperscale counterparties and underwriting; President, CEO & Director Power said AI-related wins were present in smaller deployments, noting "roughly 20%" of the $108 million in 0-1 megawatt bookings, while large footprint capacity remained focused on "traditional strong investment-grade credit names."

  • Jonathan Atkin, RBC: asked about openness to neocloud/LLM customer credit and demand vertical trends; President, CEO & Director Power said the scenario was "more hypothetical than reality" for DLR’s large deals, and Chief Revenue Officer Colin McLean pointed to a "record channel quarter" with "nearly 40% of our bookings" through channel and said interconnection is becoming a greater part of the proposition.

  • Jonathan Petersen, Jefferies: asked whether Kansas City signals a broader move into newer markets and timing; Chief Investment Officer Gregory Wright said the market "is going to quickly become the seventh largest data center market in the U.S." and that power "is starting in '28 and it's going linear from there on out."

  • Michael Ng, Goldman Sachs: asked if Singapore renewal spreads were unusual or repeatable; Chief Financial Officer Mercier said it reflected a "very supply-constrained market" and added, "while we might not see this every quarter," management sees "a healthy opportunity to reprice our contracts going forward."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive and detail-oriented, with repeated probing on multi-year growth durability, renewal pricing sustainability, development/power timing, and credit mix (e.g., "walk through some of the puts and takes" and questions on whether renewal math "gets better"). (Multiple analysts)

  • Management’s tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks and remained steady in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing execution and visibility (e.g., "our business is firing on all cylinders" and "we entered the second half of 2026 with a high degree of confidence"). (President, CEO & Director Power; Chief Financial Officer Mercier)

  • Compared with Q1, management language appeared more assertive on pricing and growth runway, anchored by higher renewal spreads and another guidance raise; analysts in Q2 focused more on multi-year mechanics, fee income scaling, and new-market expansion, versus Q1’s heavier emphasis on supply chain constraints and commencements lag. (Current vs. prior transcript)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance moved higher again: in Q1, CFO Matthew Mercier raised 2026 core FFO guidance to "$8 to $8.10 per share," while in Q2, CFO Matt Mercier raised 2026 core FFO per share guidance (excluding promote) to "$8.15 to $8.20 per share."

  • Renewal spreads were a key swing factor: Q1 reported "$193 million of renewal leases" at a blended "5%" cash increase; Q2 reported "over $261 million of renewals" with cash re-leasing spreads "over 25%." (Chief Financial Officer Mercier)

  • Backlog expanded and became more material to the investment narrative: Q1 said total backlog was "$1.8 billion"; Q2 said it reached "$1.9 billion" and highlighted scheduled commencements extending into 2027 and beyond. (Chief Financial Officer Mercier)

  • Strategic activity broadened in Q2, with management emphasizing four transactions across growth pillars and detailing planned acquisitions (Teraco stake and Columbia Capital) versus Q1’s emphasis on new market entries (e.g., Telepoint) and land/power positioning for hyperscale. (President, CEO & Director Power)

Risks and concerns

  • Management cited seasonality and investment ramps as near-term offsets: CFO Matt Mercier said Q3 core FFO excluding promote could "moderate slightly" due to "seasonal ramp in net utility and R&M expenses," "a pickup in CapEx spending," and "asset recycling activity." (Chief Financial Officer Mercier)

  • Management framed leasing and development as episodic and capacity-constrained: CEO Andrew Power said hyperscale leasing "can be episodic from quarter-to-quarter" and CFO Matt Mercier characterized constrained markets as driving repricing opportunities but not necessarily repeating at the same magnitude each quarter. (President, CEO & Director Power; Chief Financial Officer Mercier)

Final takeaway

Digital Realty’s leadership described Q2 as a record execution quarter, pairing a higher 2026 core FFO outlook (excluding promote) with record small-deployment bookings, sharply higher renewal spreads, and a larger backlog that management said supports multiple years of double-digit growth. Management also tied its longer runway narrative to increased development CapEx, expanded power access in Kansas City beginning in 2028, and a scaled private capital platform, while acknowledging quarterly variability in hyperscale leasing and a seasonal expense ramp that could moderate near-term results.

data.title: MaxLinear forecasts Q3 revenue of $210M-$220M as it raises 2026 optical data center revenue to $210M-$230M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MaxLinear (MXL) Q2 2026

Management view

  • “Our Q2 financial results highlight the exciting inflection in our business trajectory and the beginning of a multiyear growth phase for MaxLinear,” said (Co-Founder, Chairman, CEO & President Kishore Seendripu), adding, “revenue grew 55% year-on-year” and “we also returned to positive GAAP EPS of $0.02.”

  • Seendripu said “Infrastructure is now our largest revenue category and grew 145% year-on-year, driven by robust production ramps in optical data center-oriented platforms,” and added, “we are once again raising our expectations for 2026 optical data center revenue to be between $210 million to $230 million with continued growth as run rates expand into 2027.”

  • On product ramps and roadmap, Seendripu said “Keystone... continues to ramp into high-volume production at major hyperscale customers across U.S. and Asia for 400-gig and 800-gig deployments,” and said, “We expect Rushmore... to become an important optical connectivity growth driver beginning in 2027.”

  • Steven Litchfield (CFO & Chief Corporate Strategy Officer Steven Litchfield) said “Total revenue for the second quarter was $168.8 million,” with segment revenue of “approximately $85 million” infrastructure, “approximately $45 million” broadband, “approximately $24 million” connectivity, and “approximately $15 million” industrial and multimarket.

Outlook

  • Litchfield guided, “We currently expect revenue in the third quarter of '26 to be between $210 million and $220 million,” and added, “we expect to see growth from all 4 of our business segments with particular strength in infrastructure driven by data center optical interconnects.”

  • Litchfield said “we are now forecasting Q3 2026 non-GAAP gross margin to be 60% at the midpoint of our guidance range,” and guided “non-GAAP gross margin to be in the range of 58.5% and 61.5% of revenue,” alongside “Q3 non-GAAP operating expenses... $66 million to $71 million.”

  • Compared with Q1 commentary on next-generation ramps, Seendripu’s Q2 timing language shifted to “We expect Rushmore... beginning in 2027,” while in Q1 he said, “We anticipate production ramps beginning in late 2026.”

Financial results

  • Litchfield reported profitability and margins: “GAAP and non-GAAP gross margin for the second quarter were 57.8% and 59.5% of revenue,” and “GAAP EPS for Q2 2026 was $0.02 per share... Non-GAAP EPS was $0.35 per share.”

  • Litchfield reported operating structure: “Second quarter GAAP operating expenses were $101.8 million and non-GAAP operating expenses were $62.8 million,” and said non-GAAP profitability was reflected in “non-GAAP income from operations in Q2 was 22% of net revenue.”

  • Litchfield highlighted working capital and supply actions tied to demand: “In Q2, net cash flow provided in operating activities was approximately $4.8 million,” and “We exited Q2 of 2026 with approximately $93.7 million in cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash,” noting this “included a substantial prepayment of wafers supporting rising demand for our data center products for which we have increasing order backlog in the second half of the year and into 2027.”

Q&A

  • Tore Svanberg, Stifel, Nicolaus & Company, Incorporated, Research Division: “you raised the optical transceiver revenue by more than $50 million... mix between 400-gig and 800-gig.” (CEO Seendripu): “all the revenue growth we are seeing now is driven by 800 gigabit PAM4 success for us... 800 gigabit is now substantially going to be a bigger portion of our run rate revenues moving forward,” and “our customers span both U.S. and Asia.”

  • Cody Acree, The Benchmark Company, LLC, Research Division: “The increase in the optical outlook... that is all just Keystone...?” (CEO Seendripu): “That's correct... It's all driven by Keystone product family.”

  • Cody Acree, The Benchmark Company, LLC, Research Division: asked on visibility and backlog. (CFO Litchfield): “the visibility is very good... It's going out on or about 6 months anyway.”

  • Joseph Quatrochi, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: asked about broadband demand and transitions. (CFO Litchfield): “Not a whole lot of changes... we've been gaining share on some of our PON programs... telco CapEx spend continue to be good.”

  • Christopher Rolland, Susquehanna Financial Group, LLLP: asked about long-term gross margin framework. (CFO Litchfield): “I don't think the target has changed,” and “with the product mix... that's still the right goal,” while cautioning, “Right now, we're seeing lots of increases of cost... whether it be on the wafer side or just test assembly, packaging.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive and focused on sustainability of the optical ramp and model assumptions, with repeated framing such as “visibility,” “mix,” “concentration,” and long-term margin model questions.

  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and confident in Q&A, using language including (CEO Seendripu) “we are obviously very excited,” and (CFO Litchfield) “we're excited about hitting the 60% level,” while repeatedly pairing optimism with cost/supply caveats.

  • Versus Q1, management maintained similar “multiyear growth phase” framing, but Q2 responses emphasized near-term magnitude (“visibility... 6 months”) and mix shifting toward 800G.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Optical data center revenue expectations increased: in Q1 Seendripu said “increasing our expectations for 2026 optical data center revenue to $150 million to $170 million range,” while in Q2 he said “once again raising our expectations... between $210 million to $230 million.”

  • Segment mix and scale continued to shift toward infrastructure: in Q1, Litchfield said “Infrastructure revenue... approximately $63 million,” while in Q2 he said “Infrastructure revenue... approximately $85 million,” alongside Seendripu’s statement that infrastructure is “now our largest revenue category.”

  • Q&A emphasis stayed centered on optical ramps and supply; Q2 added more detailed discussion of gross margin trajectory and the durability of longer-term targets.

Risks and concerns

  • Litchfield flagged input-cost pressure and demand-fulfillment premiums: “we're seeing lots of increases of cost... wafer... test assembly, packaging,” and said, “where you're paying premiums in some cases, meeting customer demand.”

  • Management described mitigation as pricing and mix: Litchfield said, “In some cases, you can pass this along to customers,” and highlighted that “the mix of our infrastructure products historically has been well above the corporate average.”

Final takeaway

Management framed Q2 as a sustained turn into an infrastructure-led cycle, citing “a multiyear growth phase,” raising 2026 optical data center revenue expectations to “$210 million to $230 million,” and guiding Q3 revenue to “between $210 million and $220 million,” while acknowledging that wafer, packaging, and test cost inflation and supply tightness remain active considerations even as 800G Keystone drives the current ramp and Rushmore/Washington/Annapurna are positioned for initial revenue starting in 2027.

data.title: Five Point reaffirms approximately $100M 2026 net income as it expects remaining land sales in Q4
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Five Point Holdings, LLC (FPH) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO Daniel Hedigan framed the quarter around a two-part strategy: monetizing California master-planned communities while expanding fee-based earnings through Hearthstone. He said the company is “expanding our capabilities beyond land development into land banking and asset management,” and added, “together, these businesses provide multiple avenues for creating value and position us to participate more broadly in the homebuilding ecosystem.”
  • CEO Hedigan highlighted a major Great Park Venture commercial land transaction, stating: “Five Point generated net income of $29.9 million, driven in large part by the Great Park Venture sale of 17.7 acres of commercial land planned for a senior living retirement community for $159.3 million, which represents a land value of $9 million per acre.”
  • CEO Hedigan emphasized liquidity and capital structure flexibility, saying the company “ended the quarter with total liquidity of $565.9 million, including $348.4 million of cash and cash equivalents,” and “our debt-to-capital ratio stands at just 16.2%.”
  • CFO Kim Tobler detailed earnings mix and the Great Park Venture driver: “Our second quarter consolidated net income was $29.9 million,” and “The equity in earnings from the Great Park Venture resulted from the net income to this venture of $114.2 million, which was largely attributable to the land sale...that generated revenue of $159.3 million and a 76.5% gross margin.”
  • COO Michael Alvarado described softer home sales but continued builder pipeline, noting at Great Park “builders sold 56 homes...compared to 82 homes in the first quarter,” while the company has “builders in various stages of due diligence and contracts on 5 new residential programs, totaling approximately 28.5 acres.”

Outlook

  • CEO Hedigan kept guidance unchanged: “we're not going to update or alter our prior guidance of approximately $100 million in consolidated net income at this time.”
  • CEO Hedigan shifted expected land-sale timing later in the year, stating: “We currently expect our remaining land sales activity will occur in the fourth quarter.”
  • Management reiterated timing risk tied to macro factors, with CEO Hedigan adding: “we currently believe our land will sell as expected, with the caveat that interest rates and affordability factors could affect timing.”

Financial Results

  • CFO Tobler said management services revenue totaled $14.7 million, consisting of “$9.1 million” from Great Park Venture management, “$5.8 million” of Great Park incentive compensation, and “$5.6 million earned from our Hearthstone asset management platform.”
  • CFO Tobler reported management services costs and expenses of $5.6 million, including “$2.4 million...associated with the Great Park Venture, and $3.2 million associated with Hearthstone.”
  • CFO Tobler reported “$41 million of equity in earnings from our unconsolidated entities,” including “$39.7 million” from the Great Park Venture, and also stated “Our second quarter SG&A was $14.3 million.”
  • CFO Tobler said cash sources included “$79.6 million of distributions and incentive compensation payments,” and cash uses included “development costs at Valencia and San Francisco that totaled $32.2 million” and repurchasing “approximately 623,000 shares...for $3.1 million.”

Q&A

  • Alan Ratner, Zelman & Associates LLC: asked if Five Point is “hedging the timing of the next land sales” due to slower home sales and whether caution is driven by builders. President & CEO Hedigan: said the priority is “to really optimize our land value, and we're not prepared to compromise on land value,” while adding, “if I can help a builder a little bit with some structure, we're prepared to have those conversations.”
  • Alan Ratner, Zelman & Associates LLC: asked whether Hearthstone is seeing “an increase in walkaways” or requests for changed terms and how it could affect financials. CFO Tobler: said, “We're not seeing builders coming back to them generally and asking for changes in terms or anything like that,” and added, “they're still seeing good flow.”
  • Alan Ratner, Zelman & Associates LLC: asked about potential land sales for data centers across company assets. COO Alvarado: said data centers “has been a topic of conversation,” and “we are absolutely looking at that,” while cautioning, “They're not easy to execute on. They take a lot of time.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, centered on timing caution and land-banking risk, with Ratner probing whether management was “hedging the timing” and whether builders are pushing for “changes in pricing or terms.”
  • Management: slightly positive and valuation-focused in responses, emphasizing discipline and flexibility; Hedigan reiterated, “we're not prepared to compromise on land value,” while Tobler characterized Hearthstone conditions as “holding up well.”
  • Versus last quarter: management appeared more explicit about timing uncertainty, moving from Q1’s expectation of earnings weighted to “the third and fourth quarters” to Q2’s statement that “remaining land sales activity will occur in the fourth quarter.”

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q2 included a major Great Park Venture commercial land closing and related equity earnings, while Q1 management described “a relatively quiet quarter from a land sales perspective” with “a consolidated net loss of $5 million.”
  • Guidance language stayed consistent at “approximately $100 million,” but Q2 concentrated expected remaining land sales in “the fourth quarter,” whereas Q1 expected material sales in “the third and fourth quarters.”
  • Operationally, both Great Park and Valencia reported sequentially lower builder home sales in Q2 (56 and 78) versus Q1 (82 and 90), while management maintained the emphasis on pacing and transaction structuring to protect land value.
  • Analyst focus evolved from Q1 mechanics and appetite for land banking and inflation in development costs to Q2 timing of land sales, potential land-bank walkaways, and optionality around data center demand.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Hedigan cited market uncertainty and rate-driven timing risk: “market conditions remain uncertain,” and “interest rates and affordability factors could affect timing.”
  • Management repeatedly flagged timing variability in land transactions; COO Alvarado said on Great Park’s 5 programs, “market conditions could alter the timing,” and similarly for Valencia documentation “market conditions could alter the timing here as well.”
  • On data centers, COO Alvarado pointed to regulatory and execution complexity in California, saying municipalities “make them difficult to implement,” and “They take a lot of time.”

Final Takeaway

Management attributed Q2 profitability and liquidity to a large Great Park Venture commercial land sale and joint-venture cash distributions, while reaffirming “approximately $100 million” in 2026 consolidated net income and signaling that “remaining land sales activity will occur in the fourth quarter,” with timing sensitivity to interest rates and affordability. Alongside ongoing California entitlement and development progress, management presented Hearthstone as a capital-light, fee-oriented growth driver and told analysts it is “not seeing builders coming back...asking for changes in terms,” while remaining open to transaction structures that preserve land value.

data.title: Enova forecasts 30% to 35% adjusted EPS growth in 2026 as Grasshopper deal remains on track for later this year
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Enova International (ENVA) Q2 2026

Management view

  • CEO Steven Cunningham said, "In the second quarter, healthy originations growth in credit supported by a stable macro environment drove top and bottom line financial results that exceeded our expectations," and highlighted "consolidated originations 27% higher year-over-year to nearly $2.3 billion" and "revenue growth accelerated, growing 22% year-over-year to $929 million."

  • Cunningham emphasized credit improvement as a key driver, saying, "Positive credit was a key driver of our EPS growth as the consolidated net charge-off rate of 7.3% declined both sequentially and year-over-year," and described the pending Grasshopper Bank combination as a catalyst for funding and product expansion: "We expect the net synergies related to the transaction to drive adjusted EPS accretion of more than 25% once the synergies are fully realized in the first 2 years post closing."

  • CFO Scott Cornelis said, "Total company revenue of $929 million increased 22% from the second quarter of 2025," and tied results to "strong growth in originations, receivables and revenue, complemented by solid credit, operating efficiency and balance sheet flexibility."

Outlook

  • Cornelis guided, "For the third quarter, we expect consolidated revenue to be around 25% higher year-over-year with a net revenue margin in the 55% to 60% range," and added, "These expectations should lead to adjusted EPS for the third quarter of 2026 that is around 30% higher than the third quarter of 2025."

  • For full-year 2026, Cornelis raised targets, stating, "we now expect revenue growth of 20% to 25% compared to the full year 2025" and "full year 2026 adjusted EPS growth of 30% to 35%." He noted, "our 2026 financial expectations do not assume any contribution from the pending acquisition of Grasshopper Bank."

  • Versus the prior quarter’s commentary, management’s guidance language shifted from Q1’s "adjusted EPS growth of at least 25%" to Q2’s "adjusted EPS growth of 30% to 35%" and from expecting originations growth "around 20%" to revenue growth "20% to 25%."

Financial results

  • Q2 adjusted EPS was $4.31 vs. $3.96 (analysts’ estimate) and Q2 revenue was $929 million vs. $909,565,140 (analysts’ estimate).

  • Cornelis reported small business revenue of $439 million and consumer revenue of $477 million, and said the consolidated net revenue margin was 61% and "slightly better than expected."

  • On credit and risk, Cornelis said the consolidated net charge-off ratio was 7.3% and the consolidated fair value premium "remained at approximately 115%," while the COSL 30-plus day delinquency rate ended the quarter at 7.5%.

  • On expense and capital deployment, Cornelis stated marketing costs were $204 million, and said, "The current quarter includes $1.5 million of deal-related expenses associated with the pending Grasshopper acquisition." He also said, "During the second quarter, we acquired approximately 117,000 shares at a cost of approximately $19 million."

  • On liquidity and funding, Cornelis said Enova ended Q2 with "approximately $929 million of liquidity" and reported cost of funds of 8.1%.

Q&A

  • David Scharf, Citizens JMP Securities: Asked what drove the acceleration in consumer volumes; CEO Cunningham said, "we didn't lean into marketing" and attributed growth to demand and mix, noting recent growth was "more in the line of credit space" after a period when Enova "had slowed down a bit because there were some things that we didn't like," while also stating risk appetite "hasn't changed."

  • Scharf, Citizens JMP Securities: Asked whether unusually strong consumer net revenue margin should be treated as a new benchmark; CFO Scott Cornelis said consumer strength reflected "the strong growth and solid credit," but added, "within the historical ranges" it is "usually a little lower," and Cunningham said, "you shouldn't count on it being at that elevated level."

  • Scharf, Citizens JMP Securities: Asked for incremental color on the Grasshopper regulatory process; Cunningham said, "we remain in a constructive dialogue with both of the agencies" and added, "I look forward to closing later this year."

  • William Ryan, Seaport Research Partners: Asked about consumer outperformance and small business delinquency moving year-over-year; Cunningham said consumer credit has been "some of the best credit that we had seen in some time" but expects it to "settle back into those more typical ranges," while describing SMB as "remarkably stable" with net charge-offs expected to remain in the "4% to 5% range."

  • Vincent Caintic, BTIG: Pressed on marketing running higher than initial guidance and whether opportunities persist; Cunningham reiterated marketing is driven by demand and unit economics and said, "if you just take it as a percent of originations, that's probably the better way to look at it," adding that higher consumer new-customer mix contributed.

  • Caintic, BTIG: Asked about Grasshopper’s fintech-oriented product launches; Cunningham said, "Mike Butler and his team are very innovative" and framed near-term focus as "getting to the close" and then moving to "the innovation road maps."

  • Kyle Joseph, Stephens: Asked about SMB yield and cost of funds into close; Cunningham said SMB yield is "relatively stable" and "pretty close to what you're likely to see," while Cornelis said cost of funds should be "pretty flat to possibly down" with capital markets execution "pretty strong right now for us."

  • John Hecht, Jefferies: Asked about geographic and product mix shifts post-close and whether growth implies market share gains; Cunningham said the charter would enable expansion of "NetCredit within the bank" into "new geographies" and added, "it's clear that we are taking share with the growth rates that we're printing."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive but probing, focusing on sustainability of consumer acceleration, margin normalization, marketing intensity, and the Grasshopper timing and strategic value, including repeated process questions such as Scharf’s request for "any incremental color" on regulatory timing.

  • Management tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks and mostly steady in Q&A; Cunningham used clarifying language on marketing and margins, including "we didn't lean into marketing" and "you shouldn't count on it being at that elevated level," while reiterating confidence on the bank deal: "I have a lot of confidence in our application."

  • Versus Q1, the dialogue shifted away from geopolitical/energy-price uncertainty toward execution questions on consumer growth mix, margin reversion, and integration/innovation potential from Grasshopper.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q2 emphasized consumer reacceleration more directly than Q1, with Cunningham pointing to line-of-credit lapping and demand capture, whereas Q1 focused on SMB-led momentum and discussed being "more aggressive with our marketing" early in the year.

  • Guidance moved higher in Q2: Q1 framed full-year as "adjusted EPS growth of at least 25%" while Q2 moved to "adjusted EPS growth of 30% to 35%" and added a revenue growth range of "20% to 25%."

  • Analysts in Q2 pressed more on net revenue margin normalization and marketing efficiency, while Q1 questions leaned more into competition, fuel-price impacts, and customer acquisition shifts.

Risks and concerns

  • Cornelis cautioned that Q3 and full-year expectations "will depend upon the path of the macroeconomic environment and the resulting impact on demand, customer payment rates and the level timing and mix of originations growth."

  • Management flagged regulatory timing risk primarily through limited disclosure, with Cunningham saying, "I don't think there's a lot of additional color to provide beyond that" regarding the OCC and Federal Reserve review process.

Final takeaway

Management portrayed Q2 as a demand-driven growth quarter with improving consolidated credit and raised full-year targets, while continuing to position the Grasshopper Bank acquisition as a major funding-cost and geographic expansion lever that is expected to close later in 2026 and, once synergies are realized, to drive more than 25% adjusted EPS accretion in the first two years post-close.

data.title: Edwards lifts 2026 sales growth outlook to 10%-11% while reaffirming $2.95-$3.05 EPS
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Edwards Lifesciences (EW) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO Bernard Zovighian said the quarter came in ahead of plan: "We delivered stronger-than-expected second quarter sales growth of 12.5%." He tied performance to breadth across therapies and geographies, adding, "our results increasingly reflect the strength of our comprehensive portfolio with growth supported by multiple therapies across TAVR, mitral, tricuspid and surgical as well as meaningful contribution from each of our regions."
  • Zovighian reiterated long-term framing: "giving us confidence in our target of 10% total company sales growth on average over the long term" and characterized upcoming clinical/regulatory milestones, including "the finalization of updated U.S. TAVR NCD and the presentation of PROGRESS trial results" and "A new TR indication for PASCAL in the U.S."
  • In TAVR, Zovighian reported: "TAVR second quarter global sales of $1.3 billion were stronger than expected and increased 10.5% over the prior year," and said pricing held: "Average selling prices were stable globally." He also said U.S. competitiveness improved: "Edwards' competitive position in the U.S. increased modestly year-over-year."
  • In TMTT, Zovighian reported: "second quarter sales of $195.9 million, an increase of 44.8% year-over-year," and linked the upside to portfolio execution: "Growth from our differentiated TMTT platforms, PASCAL, EVOQUE and SAPIEN M3 exceeded our expectation in the quarter."
  • CFO Theodora Mistras said, "Total sales of $1.74 billion grew 12.5% year-over-year" and "Adjusted EPS in the quarter was $0.78," adding, "we are increasing our full year sales guidance for total company to 10% to 11%... and reaffirming our earnings guidance."

Outlook

  • Mistras updated full-year sales expectations: "Edwards now expects total company sales of $6.6 billion to $6.9 billion and TAVR sales of $4.75 billion to $5 billion at current exchange rates."
  • Management raised segment growth/targets while holding EPS: Zovighian said, "we are increasing our full year 2026 sales growth guidance for TAVR, TMTT and total company and reaffirming our earnings guidance," and added, "Our updated 2026 sales guidance is 10% to 11% for the company."
  • Mistras reaffirmed the full-year earnings range and described new tax assumptions: "We have confidence in delivering our previously increased full year adjusted EPS guidance of $2.95 to $3.05 despite now assuming our effective tax rate will be at the high end of our original 16% to 19% range, driven by Pillar Two impact and changes to California law restricting the usage of R&D credits."
  • For Q3, Mistras guided: "we're projecting sales of $1.63 billion to $1.71 billion" and "adjusted EPS in Q3 of $0.71 to $0.77," while warning that "Q3 underlying sales growth will be artificially lower than our first half performance" due to 2025 comparatives.

Financial Results

  • Mistras disclosed GAAP vs. non-GAAP profitability: "Adjusted EPS in the quarter was $0.78" and "Our GAAP EPS for the quarter was $0.42, primarily driven by the California R&D tax credit impact."
  • Margin and expense cadence were attributed to phasing and FX: "our adjusted gross profit margin was 77.6%, flat" and "Foreign exchange reduced our second quarter gross profit margin by 70 basis points compared to the prior year," while "Second quarter adjusted operating profit margin was 30%" and the first-half margin "benefited from the planned phasing of strategic investments in SG&A and R&D during the course of the year."
  • Cash and capital return capacity were highlighted: "approximately $2.9 billion in cash and cash equivalents" and "Edwards has approximately $1.5 billion remaining under our share repurchase authorization."

Q&A

  • Robert Marcus, JPMorgan: asked about TAVR share shift and market expansion plus which TMTT products drove upside; CEO Bernard Zovighian: "our growth rate in TAVR were very similar between U.S. and OUS" and on TMTT "the beauty of this quarter is it is altogether" across "PASCAL... EVOQUE... and M3."
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: probed why management emphasized PROGRESS heterogeneity and the business impact; CEO Zovighian: "we decided this time to split the PROGRESS presentation in 2" and on revenue impact, "we expect minimal impact in 2026" and "we will... provide a more detailed guidance... for 2027."
  • Travis Steed, BofA: pressed on "normalized seasonality" and NCD implications; CEO Zovighian: "we don't expect like last year to have a very strong summer" and CVP Daniel Lippis: the draft NCD offers opportunities including "a pathway for asymptomatic indications to be covered" and "modernization of the policy" to improve "program efficiency."
  • Pito Chickering, Deutsche Bank: asked if PASCAL tricuspid submission implied trial success and positioning; CVP Daveen Chopra: "our expectation is for a Q4 approval in the U.S." and said PASCAL’s "independent grasping" features "really make a difference" in Europe, shaping U.S. launch readiness.
  • Vijay Kumar, Evercore ISI: asked if EVOQUE is inflecting; CEO Zovighian: "we have seen an inflection in EVOQUE" and Chopra added momentum is driven by "new centers opening up" and "streamlin[ing] the whole process."
  • Joshua Jennings, TD Cowen: asked about TAVR-in-TAVR contribution timing; Lippis: "we have modeled that into our market model" but "right now, it represents a relatively small part of the market."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive, with repeated focus on durability-driven share shifts and new-growth catalysts (PROGRESS, NCD, EVOQUE, PASCAL TR), while questions probed timing and adoption mechanics.
  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and remained confident in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing durability and pacing of adoption; Zovighian said, "we are very excited about it" and later stressed, "we expect minimal impact in 2026" for PROGRESS.
  • Versus Q1, management maintained confidence but was more explicit on near-term pacing and comparables, repeatedly referencing the "high bar" for 2H and positioning NCD/PROGRESS as longer-dated catalysts.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance moved higher on sales but not on EPS: in Q2, Mistras raised full-year company sales growth to "10% to 11%" from "9% to 11%" and raised TAVR to "8% to 9%" from "7% to 9%"; Q1 guidance had been increased to "9% to 11%" company growth and "7% to 9%" TAVR, alongside the $2.95 to $3.05 adjusted EPS range.
  • Leadership transition was a key change: Q2 opened with Zovighian welcoming the new CFO, saying, "Since joining in June, Doretta has quickly demonstrated her impact," replacing Q1 CFO Scott Ullem.
  • PROGRESS discussion shifted from guarded in Q1 ("we tend not to talk about trial") to more educational in Q2, including baseline characteristics and explicit expectations on timing ("minimal impact in 2026").

Risks and Concerns

  • Mistras flagged tax and FX headwinds: "effective tax rate will be at the high end" of the "16% to 19%" range due to "Pillar Two impact" and California law changes, and she said, "If foreign exchange rates were to hold at current levels, we estimate foreign exchange to be a second half headwind of approximately $35 million."
  • Management emphasized tough comparatives and seasonality: Mistras said Q3 growth will be "artificially lower" due to 2025’s second-half strength, and Zovighian highlighted last year’s unusually strong summer creating a "very high comp 2H of 2026."

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q2 as broad-based execution across TAVR, TMTT, and Surgical, and raised full-year sales growth targets while reaffirming adjusted EPS, with near-term modeling shaped by second-half comparables, potential NCD tailwinds for asymptomatic coverage, and a longer-dated opportunity set tied to PROGRESS results, PASCAL tricuspid expansion, and continued EVOQUE and SAPIEN M3 scaling.

data.title: Nokia outlines Q3 net sales growth of 3%-7% as it plans AI-RAN pilots by end of 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Nokia (NOK) Q2 2026

Management view

  • Justin Hotard said Q2 “showed continued progress against the strategy we set at our Capital Markets Day,” adding that the company is focused on “maximizing our opportunity in the AI super cycle,” with Q2 net sales growth of 9%, gross margin expansion of 70 basis points to 46%, and operating margin expansion of 70 basis points to 9%.

  • Hotard highlighted AI & Cloud as “the strongest growth driver,” reporting AI & Cloud net sales “more than doubled year-on-year to EUR 446 million” and order intake of “EUR 2.8 billion,” while cautioning that “order patterns in this market can be lumpy, and we should not expect this level of intake every quarter,” and adding that “approximately half the order volume received in Q2 is expected to convert to revenue in the next 12 months.”

  • Hotard detailed product and portfolio moves: “we launched the industry's first commercial AI-RAN platform,” which he said “would deliver more than 100% spectral efficiency gains by 2028,” with Nokia “on track to enter pilot deployments at the end of this year and expect to be commercially available in 2027.” He also said Nokia “secured our first multi-rail ILA design win with a major customer.”

  • Marco Wiren said reporting changes followed the Inseego fixed wireless access deal: “both businesses are classified as discontinued operations,” and “in quarter 2 2026, this reporting change reduced comparable net sales by EUR 66 million and increased the comparable operating profit by EUR 13 million.”

Outlook

  • Wiren said, “there is no operational change to our comparable operating profit guidance,” and added, “we continue to track somewhat above the midpoint of our operating profit range,” with the only change described as “the technical change resulting from the move of Fixed Wireless Access and Enterprise Campus Edge into discontinued operations.”

  • For Q3, Wiren provided a sales outlook and profit phasing, stating, “we currently assume a sequential increase in net sales of between 3% and 7%,” and “for operating profit, we currently expect a result broadly similar to quarter 2 due to the phasing of software sales in mobile infrastructure between quarter 2 and quarter 3, followed by a meaningful improvement in quarter 4.”

  • Compared with Q1, Nokia’s Q2 outlook language shifted from a Q2 setup (“we currently assume a 5% to 9% sequential increase in net sales”) to specific Q3 phasing tied to Mobile Infrastructure software and “normal seasonality” into Q4.

Financial results

  • Wiren reported Q2 gross profit of “EUR 2.2 billion” and operating profit of “EUR 434 million,” and said the quarter “benefited from some software revenue recognition coming in quarter 2 instead of quarter 3,” while “higher stock-based compensation expense… represented 150 basis points headwind to our operating margin.”

  • Cash flow and balance sheet items were a notable swing versus Q1 seasonality: Wiren reported “free cash flow was negative EUR 732 million,” calling Q2 “typically the weakest quarter for cash generation,” and said Nokia ended Q2 with “a net cash position of EUR 2.8 billion.”

  • In Network Infrastructure, Wiren reported net sales growth of 12% with Optical Networks up 20% and IP Networks up 16%, and said Optical margin benefited from “both strong demand and the integration of Infinera,” while Fixed Networks declined 2% as Nokia emphasized higher-value areas (optical line terminal sales up 18% and ONT sales down 16%).

Q&A

  • Terence Tsui, Morgan Stanley: asked about the “4 new DSPs planned by the end of 2027” and whether milestones are achievable; President & CEO Hotard said Nokia and Infinera “were each building two DSPs individually,” and Nokia chose “to maintain the DSP team as is,” arguing “four unique DSPs” improve “market coverage.” Tsui follow-up on order “lumpiness”; Hotard said the focus is “order momentum… over a time period,” with “continued growth and continued demand.”

  • Simon Leopold, Raymond James: asked to “rank order and characterize supply chain risk”; Hotard called memory “most significant,” citing “constraints” and “significant change in pricing,” and said Nokia is “securing supply” and “simplifying our designs.” Leopold follow-up on “scale across market”; Hotard differentiated “scale across” from “data center interconnect,” saying both drive growth in “routing” and reinforce the optical/IP focus.

  • Sami Sarkamies, Danske Bank: asked if optical supply is “fully constrained”; Hotard said “in general, I would think of us as being constrained,” and added, “if there was more supply, I think we'd probably generate more revenue.” Sarkamies follow-up on radio share gains; Hotard said, “it's timing,” referencing software revenue recognition.

  • Alexander Duval, Goldman Sachs: asked about AI-RAN timing and switching; Hotard said switching “orders… come in Q2,” and on AI-RAN reiterated “pilots at the end of '26, commercially available in '27,” with “more significant volume going into '28.”

  • Ulrich Rathe, Bernstein: raised risk of “double ordering”; Hotard cited customer sophistication and said component progress is shared “transparently,” also noting longer-term commitments. Rathe follow-up on “normal length of an order book”; Hotard said typically orders were “within 12 months,” and the shift reflects AI & Cloud exposure and supply constraints.

  • Richard Kramer, Arete: pressed on an industry shift away from proprietary baseband silicon and implications for R&D and margins; Hotard said “the industry has to transition” and called “a shift to general purpose silicon” on baseband “a very clear industry shift,” noting the AI-RAN stack is “a single software stack.” Kramer follow-up on Q4 profit drivers; CFO Wiren cited Q4 telco seasonality, China integration acceleration, Europe efficiency programs, and maintained, “I don't see any issues” on cash.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on sustainability of AI & Cloud orders and supply constraints, with repeated probing on “lumpy” orders, “double ordering,” and whether Nokia is “fully constrained.”

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more technical/defensive in Q&A on constraints and order quality, with Hotard repeatedly framing variability as structural (“order patterns… can be lumpy”) while reaffirming timelines (“pilots at the end of this year,” “commercially available in 2027”).

  • Versus Q1, the Q2 exchange placed more weight on supply bottlenecks (memory, indium phosphide capacity timing) and order duration, while Q1 questions centered more on guidance uplift drivers and lead times.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Strategy emphasis intensified from Q1’s demand acceleration framing to Q2 execution and capital allocation moves, including Hotard’s announcement of an NXP Arizona manufacturing site acquisition to “increase our indium phosphide fab capacity.”

  • Q2 introduced a major reporting shift: CFO Wiren said Fixed Wireless Access and Enterprise Campus Edge moved to discontinued operations, with quantified Q2 impacts (EUR 66 million net sales reduction and EUR 13 million operating profit increase on a comparable basis).

  • Q1 highlighted strong cash generation (free cash flow of EUR 629 million); Q2 emphasized expected seasonal cash weakness (negative EUR 732 million) and reiterated the cash profile as seasonal and working-capital driven.

Risks and concerns

  • Hotard identified supply chain constraints as a gating factor, highlighting memory shortages and pricing impacts, and said lead times are elongating, requiring “better visibility, better planning” with telco customers.

  • Hotard said optical is broadly constrained on “leading-edge products,” and described forecasting as limited to “demand that we have line of sight to shipping,” with risk if “continuity of supply” breaks.

  • Wiren said increased restructuring and growth preparation shift free cash flow expectations: Nokia now expects to “track towards the low end” of its “free cash flow conversion assumption of 55% to 75%.”

Final takeaway

Management described Q2 as a step-up in AI & Cloud traction, with AI & Cloud net sales more than doubling to EUR 446 million and EUR 2.8 billion in AI & Cloud orders, while repeatedly cautioning that orders can be lumpy and are increasingly extending beyond a 12-month horizon under supply constraints. Nokia paired the demand narrative with concrete portfolio and capacity actions—AI-RAN pilots targeted for end-2026 with commercial availability in 2027, incremental U.S. optical manufacturing expansion including an NXP Arizona site acquisition, and a sharper capital allocation focus via discontinued-operation classification tied to the Inseego deal—while guiding Q3 for 3%-7% sequential net sales growth and profit phasing tied to Mobile Infrastructure software timing and Q4 seasonality.

data.title: SkyWest projects 2026 GAAP EPS in the $11 area while outlining $700M CapEx
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SkyWest (SKYW) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “Today, SkyWest reported net income of $101 million or $2.54 per diluted share for the second quarter of 2026.” (CEO, President & Director Russell A. Childs)

  • “Today, we're pleased to announce an agreement with American for SkyWest to purchase and operate 11 new E175s with deliveries beginning this year.” (CEO, President & Director Childs)

  • “We announced today that SkyWest Board of Directors has also approved a $250 million increase to our existing stock repurchase program.” (CEO, President & Director Childs)

  • “Total Q2 revenue of $1.1 billion is up 9% from $1 billion in Q1 2026 on strong block hour demand from our partners during a volatile quarter and is up 7% from $1 billion in Q2 2025.” (Chief Financial Officer Robert Simmons)

  • “We anticipate converting 4 to 6 aircraft per month starting this fall.” (President & COO – SkyWest Airlines Wade Steel)

Outlook

  • “For the full year 2026, we expect to see block hour production up approximately 5% from 2025.” (CFO Simmons)

  • “We anticipate our GAAP EPS for 2026 will be in the $11 area, subject to ongoing prorate fuel volatility.” (CFO Simmons)

  • “This assumes average jet fuel of $3.65 per gallon for the second half of 2026 on 28 million gallons of jet fuel needed in the second half for our prorate business.” (CFO Simmons)

  • “We anticipate our total CapEx in 2026 will be approximately $700 million.” (CFO Simmons)

Financial Results

  • “Today, we reported a second quarter GAAP net income of $101 million or $2.54 earnings per share.” (CFO Simmons)

  • “Q2 pretax income was $139 million, 29% higher than Q1 pretax income on solid demand for our various contract and prorate products and sequential seasonality.” (CFO Simmons)

  • “Q2 revenue includes contract revenue of $864 million, prorate and charter revenue of $201 million and leasing and other revenue was $38 million.” (CFO Simmons)

  • “Our price per gallon on our prorate flying was $4.45 in Q2, up from $2.88 in Q2 2025 and up from $3.40 in Q1.” (CFO Simmons)

  • “The ending cash balance for the quarter included the effects from: one, repaying $122 million in debt; ... three, investing $139 million in CapEx, including the purchase of 1 E175; and four, buying back 833,000 shares of SkyWest stock in Q2 for $75 million.” (CFO Simmons)

Q&A

  • Savanthi Syth, Raymond James: “Are you having any kind of different conversations with partners?”; CEO Childs: “I think we've reflected that in our script... we're pressing forward quite strong with our partners and good conversations about strategies to continue to enhance value to them.”

  • Savanthi Syth, Raymond James: “Is there just more opportunity on the prorate side versus charter?”; President Steel: “the demand, as you know, for charter is pretty light in the summertime... we're seeing very strong demand in the prorate side.”

  • Michael Linenberg, Deutsche Bank: “how should we think about the improvement in profitability?”; President Steel: “the profitability of the E175s will be very consistent with our other fleets that we have.”

  • Michael Linenberg, Deutsche Bank: “How should we think about your debt profile?”; CFO Simmons: “we'll be financing 11 of the new E175s this year and adding new debt for that. But we do expect that debt will continue to trend down over the next several years.”

  • Duane Pfennigwerth, Evercore ISI: “pacing of the buyback going forward”; CFO Simmons: “we try to maintain a balanced approach but remain opportunistic.”

  • Catherine O'Brien, Goldman Sachs: “any other puts and takes on flying for the rest of the year”; President Steel: “they'll have very little impact on the block hours in 2026... we are confident that we are going to increase year-over-year 5% in our block hours.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, centered on fuel volatility, capacity cadence, and capital return pacing, including “suddenly a sharp rising fuel price” (Savanthi Syth, Raymond James) and “It's a little surprising you could find slots within the year” (Duane Pfennigwerth, Evercore ISI).

  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, emphasizing demand and execution, including “we remain well positioned to adapt quickly” (CEO, President & Director Childs) and “we are confident... we do not need the American airplanes to hit that 5%” (President Steel).

  • Versus last quarter: management’s tone on production appeared firmer, shifting from “we expect our block hours to be slightly less than what we talked about last quarter” (Chief Commercial Officer Steel, Q1) to “we are confident that we are going to increase year-over-year 5% in our block hours” (President Steel, Q2).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • New in Q2: “an agreement with American for SkyWest to purchase and operate 11 new E175s” (CEO, President & Director Childs) and “a $250 million increase to our existing stock repurchase program” (CEO, President & Director Childs).

  • Guidance framing: Q1 emphasized downside uncertainty—“we now expect to see block hour production slightly lower this summer than we modeled last quarter” (CFO Simmons, Q1)—while Q2 reiterated a specific full-year growth view—“block hour production up approximately 5% from 2025” (CFO Simmons, Q2).

  • Fleet strategy continuity with added detail in Q2: Q1 previewed the CRJ450—“we are excited to share a prototype of the new CRJ450 product” (CEO, President & Director Childs, Q1)—and Q2 added conversion cadence—“convert 4 to 6 aircraft per month starting this fall” (President Steel, Q2).

Risks and Concerns

  • Fuel volatility remained a core swing factor, with management stating results “were affected by higher fuel costs impact our prorate business” (CEO, President & Director Childs) and 2026 EPS is “subject to ongoing prorate fuel volatility” (CFO Simmons).

  • Maintenance network constraints persisted: “We continue to face challenges in our third-party MRO network, including labor and part shortages.” (President Steel)

  • Fleet transitions could pressure mix: “These gains are partially offset by the gradual return of approximately 19 lower-margin Delta-owned CRJ900s to Delta over the next couple of years.” (President Steel)

Final Takeaway

SkyWest’s management framed Q2 around stronger demand-driven production, a new 11-aircraft E175 agreement with American, and expanded capital return capacity via a $250 million repurchase authorization increase. For 2026, management reiterated block hours “up approximately 5%” and GAAP EPS “in the $11 area,” while highlighting prorate fuel volatility and ongoing MRO constraints as key variables alongside an active fleet transition plan anchored by E175 deliveries and CRJ450 conversions.

data.title: Intel forecasts Q3 2026 EPS of $0.38 on $15.8B-$16.8B revenue while planning 2026 CapEx above $20B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Intel (INTC) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “Q2 was another quarter of solid execution. Revenue, gross margin, earnings per share were above our guidance... Strong demand for our products continue to outpace our growing supply.” (CEO & Director Lip-Bu Tan)

  • “Our recent announcement to deepen our collaboration with Google Cloud will help to accelerate the transformation as we fully embrace an AI-first mentality throughout operations.” (CEO & Director Tan)

  • “During Q2, our factories across Intel 7, Intel 3 and Intel 18A exceeded internal volume targets, driven by improving yields, better cycle times and increasing wafer starts... We also began risk production of 18A-P.” (CEO & Director Tan)

  • “We remain on track for 14A risk production for our internal products in second half of 2027, and we make the decision in Q2 to fully committed to high-volume ramp in 2028.” (CEO & Director Tan)

  • “Second quarter revenue was $16.1 billion... Q2 non-GAAP gross margin was 41.8%... We delivered second quarter non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.42... Q2 operating cash flow was $7 billion... approximately $30 billion in cash and short-term investments.” (Executive VP, CFO David Zinsner)

  • “Due to strong customer demand signals, we're raising our outlook for 2026 and now expect our CapEx to be more than $20 billion.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

Outlook

  • “We're guiding Q3 revenue to a range of $15.8 billion to $16.8 billion... At the midpoint of $16.3 billion, we forecast gross margin of 42%, a tax rate of 11% and EPS of $0.38, all on a non-GAAP basis.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • “We continue to tightly manage non-GAAP operating expenses to roughly $16.5 billion for the year.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • Q2 vs. Q1 guidance language shifted toward larger investment needs: Q1 said 2026 CapEx would be “flat to last year,” while Q2 said 2026 CapEx is now expected to be “more than $20 billion.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • Demand and constraints remained the key framing: “Industry-wide supply constraints across wafers, memory and substrates remain the dominant challenge.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

Financial Results

  • “Second quarter revenue was $16.1 billion... Q2 non-GAAP gross margin was 41.8%... non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.42.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • Segment details highlighted a sharper sequential step-up: “CCPG revenue was $8.9 billion, up 15% sequentially... DCAI revenue was $6.3 billion, an increase of 24% sequentially and 59% year-over-year... Intel Foundry, revenue of $5.8 billion was up 6% sequentially... External foundry revenue was $293 million.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • Foundry profitability remained negative but improved: “Intel foundry operating loss in Q2 was $2.1 billion and $348 million better quarter-over-quarter.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • Mix and cost actions were central to margins: “The upside was driven by higher revenue, better yields and higher ASPs due to mix and pricing actions.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

Q&A

  • Benjamin Reitzes, Melius Research LLC: asked what the higher 2026 and 2027 CapEx implies for foundry customers and packaging. Executive VP, CFO Zinsner said, “The CapEx is fairly broad-based... include advanced packaging... skewed towards the front end,” adding, “this increased investment is a signal of our confidence in customers across all of our business units.”

  • Reitzes, Melius Research LLC: asked about a competitor’s CPU TAM projection and Intel’s ability to fulfill demand. Executive VP, CFO Zinsner responded, “without putting a number out there... we feel like the growth is going to be significant,” and said Intel sees CPU-to-GPU “almost in parity at this point.”

  • Joseph Moore, Morgan Stanley: asked about server share prospects vs. AMD and ARM. CEO & Director Tan answered, “we have a strong road map... Clearwater Forest, Diamond Rapid and also Coral Rapid with the SMT involved,” and added, “Some areas we are still behind, but we are catching up very fast.”

  • Stacy Rasgon, Bernstein: pressed on surprising client strength and second-half client expectations. Executive VP, CFO Zinsner said client upside was “largely ASP,” and guided near-term shape: “client will be flattish... Q4... will be soft,” while adding, “we'll pivot as much of the production as possible over to CPUs and data centers.”

  • Timothy Arcuri, UBS: asked if late-Q3 capacity implies a Q4 step-up. Executive VP, CFO Zinsner said, “we only guide 1 quarter out,” but added, “we start to see some of the logjam break towards the end of the third quarter... see upside in the fourth quarter,” while noting, “we will not catch up.”

  • Vivek Arya, BofA Securities: asked when external foundry confidence will be backed by customer announcements and how 2027 CapEx should be framed. CEO & Director Tan tied confidence to milestones: “PDK 0.9 for 14A is on track for October... risk production for 14A will be second half of 2027.” Executive VP, CFO Zinsner added, “We'll increase tooling in '26 by 40% relative to '25,” and said on 2027, “we need another quarter or 2 to really solidify the number.”

  • Arya, BofA Securities: asked about funding the stepped-up investment. Executive VP, CFO Zinsner said, “We have over $30 billion of cash... a $10 billion revolver,” and added, “we have... roughly... $10 billion of... noncore assets that can still be monetized,” while also noting, “we may need to tap the capital markets.”

  • Aaron Rakers, Wells Fargo: asked about ASIC growth and margins, and about Intel’s role in memory. Executive VP, CFO Zinsner said, “we're probably running at about a $2 billion run rate... we think... we'll be at a $4 billion run rate.” CEO & Director Tan said on memory, “we hired Seok-Hee Lee... memory... becomes the bottleneck,” adding, “Stay tuned.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on sustainability and transparency, focusing on CapEx magnitude/timing, supply bottlenecks, client durability, and share regain (e.g., “when will that confidence be backed by actual customer announcements?”). (Vivek Arya, BofA Securities)

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing constraints and execution (e.g., “we will not catch up”). (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • Versus Q1, management language sounded more confident on demand and investment posture (Q2: “raising our outlook for 2026” CapEx; Q1: CapEx “flat”), while analyst skepticism shifted from validating demand to probing how quickly Intel can add capacity.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 increased the scale of the investment message: Q1 framed 2026 CapEx as “flat,” while Q2 raised it to “more than $20 billion,” and added that 2027 CapEx is expected “significantly above the 2026 levels.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • Q2 provided firmer foundry timeline commitments: Q1 said it expected “earlier design commitments... beginning in the second half of 2026,” while Q2 emphasized 14A schedule milestones and a decision “to fully committed to high-volume ramp in 2028.” (CEO & Director Tan)

  • Analyst focus was consistent on supply, CapEx, and competitive positioning; Q2 added more pressure on client strength surprise and the mechanics of gross margin normalization.

Risks and Concerns

  • “Industry-wide supply constraints across wafers, memory and substrates remain the dominant challenge.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • PC demand risk was reiterated: “we expect PC consumption to be subseasonal in the second half of the year and down low double digits percent for all of 2026, impacted by rising memory prices and constraints.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • Execution and ramp risk remained embedded in margin commentary, with management highlighting early-life-cycle mix effects and supply-chain chokepoints beyond wafers (substates, memory, “T-glass”). (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

Final Takeaway

Intel’s Q2 message centered on demand outstripping supply across CPUs and foundry services, with management tying improved execution on Intel 18A, risk production of 18A-P, and concrete Intel 14A milestones to a materially higher investment plan. Management guided Q3 to $15.8B-$16.8B revenue and $0.38 non-GAAP EPS, while describing supply-chain bottlenecks (wafers, substrates, memory) as the main limiter and positioning higher 2026 CapEx (above $20B) as demand-backed and disciplined, with potential additional funding levers including customer prepays and, if needed, capital markets access.

data.title: Knowles forecasts 10%-12% 2026 revenue growth as defense demand drives multiyear orders into 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Knowles Corporation (KN) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “We had another quarter of strong broad-based organic growth as we continue to build on the momentum we saw in the first quarter,” said Jeffrey Niew (President, CEO & Director), adding, “we are beginning to see positive momentum in some of our new growth platforms and markets that bodes well to drive additional growth in 2027 and beyond.”

  • Niew (CEO) highlighted segment demand and order activity, including: “In the defense market, our RF microwave products continue to support strong growth across many communications applications,” and “we are seeing more defense customers coming to us, wanting to place multiyear orders to secure capacity.”

  • On large orders and visibility, Niew (CEO) said, “early in July, we received a $15 million-plus order for a radar application that is expected to ship over 36 months starting in 2027,” and added, “we intend to continue to call these large multiyear orders as we receive them.”

  • Niew (CEO) emphasized Precision Devices ordering strength: “Overall, book-to-bill in Precision Devices was 1.4… Orders in the PD segment were nearly $140 million in Q2… providing me confidence in continued growth in the future.”

  • John Anderson (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer) summarized profitability and cash generation: “We reported second quarter revenues of $167 million… EPS was $0.33… Cash provided by operating activities was $28 million,” and added, “we repurchased 416,000 shares at a total cost of $15 million.”

Outlook

  • Management raised its full-year growth view, with Niew (CEO) stating, “we now expect our revenue growth in 2026 to be between 10% and 12%, well above the high end of our organic revenue growth target of 4% to 6% that we provided at our Investor Day in May of last year.”

  • Anderson (CFO) provided Q3 guidance: “For the third quarter of 2026, revenues are expected to be between $167 million and $177 million… We are projecting EPS to be within the range of $0.34 to $0.38 per share,” and “we’re projecting cash from operating activities to be within the range of $35 million to $45 million.”

  • On the implied Q4 setup and segment mix, Niew (CEO) told Christopher Rolland, Susquehanna Financial Group, LLLP, Research Division: “we’re going to see sequential growth from Q3 to Q4,” while also noting, “we have a little bit of a headwind in our MSA segment in Q4… inventory building in the first half of the year.”

Financial Results

  • The company reported Q2 performance (non-GAAP continuing operations basis): revenue of $167 million, EPS of $0.33, and cash from operating activities of $28 million.

  • Segment results included Medtech & Specialty Audio revenue of $69 million with gross margin of 53.1%, and Precision Devices revenue of $98 million with segment gross margin of 40.1%.

  • Cost and balance-sheet items called out by Anderson (CFO) included R&D expense of $9 million, SG&A expense of $31 million, interest expense of $2 million, capital spending of $7 million, quarter-end cash of $50 million, and $131 million of borrowings outstanding under the revolving credit facility.

Q&A

  • Christopher Rolland, Susquehanna Financial Group, LLLP, Research Division: asked about moving parts behind full-year 10% to 12% growth and Q4 cadence; President Niew: “we’re going to see sequential growth from Q3 to Q4,” and “the PD segment will continue to grow at the rates that are similar to what we said,” while adding he had “not been able to fully digest” new hearing-aid industry data that “could be closer to the 4% range” for the end market.

  • Rolland, Susquehanna Financial Group, LLLP, Research Division: asked whether passive-market tightness is influencing Knowles and whether pricing could be higher in 2H; President Niew: “Demand is definitely stronger in the back half of the year than we would have projected at the beginning of the year,” and “the pricing environment is definitely stronger than it was last year, and we’re probably going to get more pricing this year.”

  • Bob Labick, CJS Securities, Inc.: asked what is driving broad Precision Devices growth beyond the energy shipments and about the cadence for the rest of the year; President Niew: “it’s very broad-based,” cautioned about not adding capacity for “transient orders,” and said, “Bookings are strong again in July.”

  • Labick, CJS Securities, Inc.: asked about M&A posture given organic strength; President Niew: “we’re being very selective in M&A,” called the Cornell deal “a home run,” and said, “if we find the right deal, we will move forward on the right deal,” while Anderson (CFO) added, “absent M&A, we’ll continue… buying back shares, and we still have some debt to pay down.”

  • Anthony Stoss, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC, Research Division: asked for defense mix and multiyear visibility; Anderson (CFO) said, “Filter is roughly $80 million, 90% of that is defense,” and Niew (CEO) said customers are signaling “significant increases in volume over the next 24 to 36 months” and cited the “$15 million” radar order starting shipments in 2027.

  • Stoss, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC, Research Division: asked about PD margin trajectory; Anderson (CFO) said, “we should be in the low 40% range in that PD segment in the back half of the year,” and Niew (CEO) added that pricing actions “don’t really hit the P&L until next year.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive tone, with questions focused on durability of the raised full-year growth view, PD demand breadth, pricing leverage, defense visibility, and the pace of margin expansion (e.g., Stoss: “more bullish on the military defense… What kind of visibility?”).

  • Management: positive in prepared remarks and confident in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing breadth and durability of demand (e.g., Niew (CEO): “Orders are super high,” and Anderson (CFO): “our… results… combined with the robust backlog… give me confidence”).

  • Versus last quarter, management language shifted from expecting growth “above the high end” of targets in Q1 to a tighter, explicit full-year revenue growth range in Q2 (“between 10% and 12%”), while analyst questioning shifted more toward Q4 cadence and pricing strength as demand tightened.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance framing tightened quarter over quarter: in Q1, Niew (CEO) said 2026 revenue growth would be “above the high end” of the 4% to 6% target; in Q2, he provided a specific 2026 range of “between 10% and 12%.”

  • Precision Devices order commentary strengthened: Q1 book-to-bill was “1.19”; Q2 book-to-bill increased to “1.4,” alongside explicit discussion of multiyear defense orders, including a “$15 million-plus” radar order shipping over “36 months starting in 2027.”

  • Q&A emphasis evolved from energy-ramp execution and capacity utilization discussions in Q1 to broader industry tightness, incremental pricing leverage, and more detailed defense mix and visibility in Q2.

Risks and Concerns

  • Demand pacing risk in Medtech & Specialty Audio was acknowledged, with Niew (CEO) pointing to “a little bit of a headwind in our MSA segment in Q4” tied to “inventory building in the first half of the year,” while reiterating “no real problems there, 2% to 4% for the full year.”

  • Capacity and order-quality discipline was a repeated theme, with Niew (CEO) cautioning against expanding for “transient orders” driven by broader industry lead-time dislocations.

  • Execution risk around expanded PD capacity investments was flagged indirectly through planned spend, with Niew (CEO) saying the company is “looking at taking up capacity in a number of areas in the PD segment for 2027,” while keeping CapEx “at that 5% of revenue.”

Final Takeaway

Management described Q2 as a continuation of “strong broad-based organic growth,” paired with a more explicit full-year growth framework of 10% to 12% for 2026 and Q3 guidance calling for revenue of $167 million to $177 million and EPS of $0.34 to $0.38. In Q&A, leadership tied durability to a strong Precision Devices booking environment, increasing pricing tailwinds, and improving defense visibility—highlighted by multiyear customer behavior and a $15 million-plus radar order beginning shipments in 2027—while acknowledging a Q4 MSA headwind linked to earlier inventory building.
